main_summary.summary: Dr. William G. Anderson, osteopathic physician and founding president of the Albany Movement, was interviewed July 26, 2011 in Detroit by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program for the Civil Rights History Project. Anderson recounts growing up in Americus, Georgia in a household that prized education, serving in the last all-Black company in the U.S. Navy during World War II, and pursuing medical training at the Des Moines Still College of Osteopathic Medicine after being denied admission by allopathic schools. He describes his 1957 return to Albany to practice medicine alongside Dr. William L. Reese, the city's reigning segregation under Mayor Asa Kelly and police chief Laurie Pritchett, and his decision in 1961 to organize a coalition of the Criterion Club, NAACP, Urban League and other groups into the Albany Movement after SNCC organizers Charles Sherrod and Cordell Reagon arrived. Anderson recounts inviting Martin Luther King Jr. and Ralph David Abernathy to a Shiloh Baptist mass meeting, the marches that filled Albany's jails, his wife's arrest, the city's reneging on a verbal biracial-committee agreement negotiated through attorney Donald L. Hollowell, the appeal before Judge Elbert Tuttle argued by Constance Baker Motley, and his later visiting-faculty series at Michigan State University.
key_themes: Black osteopathic medical practice in the Jim Crow South
World War II Navy service and the integrated all-Black test company
SCLC-SNCC coalition tensions in a local campaign
Spousal partnership in civil rights leadership
main_summary.historical_significance:
Anderson's testimony is the only first-person account in the CRHP collection from the elected president of the Albany Movement, providing direct narration of how the December 1961 mass mobilization was organized through pre-existing Black civic infrastructure (Criterion Club, NAACP, Urban League, women's clubs) at Ed Hamilton's home, and how SCLC was invited into the campaign under negotiated terms with SNCC. He describes specifics rarely preserved elsewhere: drawing straws in jail to decide who would leave to appear on Lawrence Spivak's Meet the Press, the verbal-only nature of the abortive biracial-committee agreement, and his interaction with a National Guardsman who lowered his rifle when Anderson volunteered to be struck first.

Anderson also offers a sustained study of Laurie Pritchett's evolution from segregation-era police chief to personal friend, complicating the standard narrative of Albany as Martin Luther King Jr.'s political failure. His account of Pritchett's restraint relative to Bull Connor's Birmingham violence, and of the city's strategy of closing every public facility rather than desegregating, gives historians a primary source on how local white officials adapted to mass nonviolent pressure.

chapters.title: Americus and a College-Bound Family
chapters.summary: Anderson describes growing up in Americus, Georgia in a farming community of 5,000-6,000, with a father who attended Morehouse and a mother who earned her B.S. degree over twenty summers while teaching school. Both parents insisted all three children would go to college. He recounts the totally segregated school system, used books, the buzzard-roost movie balcony, and being told to get off the sidewalk by white pedestrians.

chapters.title: Fort Valley State and Horace Mann Bond
chapters.summary: Anderson enters Fort Valley State College at age 15, working multiple jobs at $25-a-month tuition. He becomes personal driver for college president Horace Mann Bond, father of Julian Bond, traveling with him through Georgia, Alabama and Mississippi as Bond studied Black education in the South.

chapters.title: Pearl Harbor and Navy Service
chapters.summary: After Pearl Harbor, Anderson attempts to enlist at 14 and finally joins the Navy at 16 as a Seaman rather than a Steward. His all-Black company, composed entirely of high school graduates, was the last segregated Navy company and the first to integrate a battalion at Great Lakes. He served in the Seventh Fleet near Leyte Gulf and trained as a hospital corpsman in six weeks in the Philippines, confirming his ambition to become a doctor.

chapters.title: Mortuary School, Marriage, and Meeting King
chapters.summary: Denied admission to medical schools because Fort Valley lacked accreditation, Anderson moves to Atlanta to use GI Bill benefits, attends Mortuary Science School with curricula similar to pre-med, and meets the woman who becomes his wife. Her brother is a close friend of high-school-senior Martin Luther King Jr. Anderson then takes a job at a Montgomery funeral home owned by Loveless, attends Alabama State College for Negroes, and meets schoolmate Ralph David Abernathy, who became godfather to Anderson's oldest child.

chapters.title: Des Moines Still College of Osteopathic Medicine
chapters.summary: Through his father's friend Dr. William L. Reese, an osteopathic physician in Albany with the largest practice in town, Anderson is admitted to the Des Moines Still College of Osteopathic Medicine in 1952. He works as a radio announcer with Alley Pat Patrick at WERD in Atlanta and continues radio work in Des Moines, transforming the station's one-hour ethnic-music block into a four-hour nightly program called Gala Time while serving as a mortician preparing cadavers for the medical school.

chapters.title: Race in Des Moines
chapters.summary: Anderson recounts being denied admission to the campus Masons because he came from a 'clandestine' Black Masonic organization, and being warned by Dean John B. Schumaker not to socialize with white women. He defies the warning to dance with the white chemistry-department chair at an official school function. He describes Babe's Pizza refusing to serve Blacks and the Des Moines YMCA being one of the few unsegregated institutions, a fact that shaped his lifelong YMCA fundraising.

chapters.title: Flint Internship and Return to Albany
chapters.summary: Anderson becomes the first Black to receive training outside a college-owned hospital in U.S. history, interning at Flint Osteopathic Hospital in 1955 after Dean Schumaker personally vouches for him to administrator Keith Balker. Flint surgeons D'Aderian and Taviner offer to set him up in practice, but his wife reminds him of his pledge to return south. Through friends Bird and Ginger Edwards, the couple decides to relocate. C.B. King and Dr. William L. Reese in Albany convince him to settle there in 1957.

chapters.title: Practicing Medicine in Albany
chapters.summary: Anderson opens his practice charging $3 office visits, $5 house calls, and $50 deliveries, accepting hams, chickens, and eggs in payment. He sees up to 100 patients a day and observes Black patients being warehoused on mercurial diuretics when whole-leaf digitalis was the proper treatment. He describes the Ku Klux Klan holding rallies one block from his home, traveling to Bainbridge through Baker County with a pistol on the seat after pastor E. James Grant of Mount Zion warned him of Newton's lynching reputation.
related_events: Black healthcare in the Jim Crow South

chapters.title: Founding the Albany Movement
chapters.summary: When SNCC organizers Charles Sherrod and Cordell Reagon begin voter-registration work in Albany in late 1961, Anderson watches from his office as young volunteers are harassed. He convenes the Criterion Club, NAACP, Urban League, the Uralis, and other groups at Ed Hamilton's home and is elected president of what becomes the Albany Movement. He invites Martin Luther King Jr. and Abernathy to Shiloh Baptist; King speaks four times in one night between Shiloh and Mount Zion across the street.
related_events: Charles Sherrod and Cordell Reagon arrival

chapters.title: Marches, Arrests, and Family Sacrifice
chapters.summary: Anderson describes the gendered emotional architecture of the movement and his wife's role as the more politically committed partner. He recounts the first major march in which she was arrested and held at the Lee County prison farm while he slept in different homes nightly to evade the Klan, and the Chatwell family caring for his four children. He reflects on operating on nerves and emotion rather than intellect when facing the Ku Klux Klan.

chapters.title: Meet the Press and the Failed Settlement
chapters.summary: Anderson describes the march in which a National Guardsman lowered his rifle after Anderson said 'if you hit anybody in this line, hit me first.' He, King and Abernathy were jailed together. Lawrence Spivak called repeatedly demanding King appear on Meet the Press; the three drew straws and Anderson drew the short one, was briefed in New York by Clarence B. Jones, and appeared on the program. Attorney Donald L. Hollowell accepted a verbal-only biracial-committee agreement that the city promptly broke, closing every public facility rather than desegregating.

chapters.title: Constance Baker Motley and Judge Tuttle
chapters.summary: Anderson recounts the appeal of Judge J. Robert Elliott's injunction before Judge Elbert Tuttle in Atlanta, where Constance Baker Motley argued without notes and refuted every case cited by lawyers from Emory and the University of Georgia. He disputes Coretta Scott King's published characterization of King's compliance with the injunction as the moment that broke the movement, arguing instead that the city's reneging on the agreement was the turning point but that the campaign succeeded in changing white perceptions and Black self-conception.

chapters.title: Pritchett's Friendship and Albany's Transformation
chapters.summary: Anderson describes his post-movement friendship with Laurie Pritchett, whom he assesses as a fundamentally decent man trapped by the City Council and who refused to deploy Bull Connor-style violence. He recounts revisiting Albany decades later with white friend Rhea Hyle, seeing the federal court building named after C.B. King, the mayor and sheriff Black, and staying in the Windsor Hotel penthouse suite in Americus where he had once worked as a bellhop at 13.

chapters.title: Michigan State Visiting Faculty Series
chapters.summary: Anderson concludes by describing the visiting-minority-faculty series he has produced at Michigan State University for twelve years, bringing distinguished African American civil rights veterans to campus each Black History Month. Guests have included Wyatt Tee Walker, Juanita Abernathy, Joseph E. Lowery, Bernard LaFayette, Dorothy Cotton, Shirley Sherrod, and contemporary scholars including Rev. Charles G. Adams.
